justification: All 7 rubric criteria met: (1) 7 business rules identified — KYC gate (BR-1), daily limits (BR-2), post-settlement irreversibility (BR-3), sanctions screening (BR-4), login lockout (BR-5), notification threshold (BR-6), unverified receive-only (BR-7). (2) Notification rule (BR-6) correctly marked invariant: false with clear justification. (3) Sanctions rule (BR-4) marked invariant: true with regulatory justification. (4) Timezone ambiguity for daily limit explicitly surfaced in both low_confidence_areas and escalations. (5) 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria, well exceeding the 4 minimum. (6) Confidence level 72, within the 60-85 range, with 6 assumptions listed.

observations.text: Invariant classifications are nuanced — BR-2 (daily limits) correctly marked invariant: false because the specific dollar amounts are policy, while noting the existence of limits is regulatory. This distinction exceeds rubric expectations.
12 acceptance criteria with strong edge case coverage including counter-reset on successful login (AC-12), locked account with correct password (AC-9), and boundary limit testing (AC-3, AC-4).

Identified multiple ambiguities beyond the required timezone gap: partially-verified vs unverified KYC step definitions, sanctions screening timing, account lock interaction with password reset, and partially-verified withdrawal eligibility.
Edge cases are well-reasoned — the $1,000 boundary interpretation (strictly greater-than), cumulative vs per-transaction notification, and the settlement-status-not-clock distinction for reversals.
Escalations include concrete options and recommendations, not just flagged gaps — e.g., recommending UTC calendar day for daily limits with rationale.

BR-1 combines both KYC-before-sending and unverified-receive-only into one rule, while BR-7 restates the receive aspect. Could be cleaner as: BR-1 = KYC required before send/withdraw, BR-7 = something distinct. Minor structural redundancy.
